Insiders invest with the hopes of seeing their money grow in value over time. However, as a result of recent losses, their initial investment is now only worth US$184.6k, which is not what they expected.

As a general rule, we feel more positive about a stock when an insider has bought shares at above current prices, because that suggests they viewed the stock as good value, even at a higher price. data.title: We're Not Very Worried About Forte Minerals' (CSE:CUAU) Cash Burn Rate
data.description:
We can readily understand why investors are attracted to unprofitable companies. Indeed, Forte Minerals (CSE:CUAU) stock is up 177% in the last year, providing strong gains for shareholders. Nonetheless, only a fool would ignore the risk that a loss making company burns through its cash too quickly.

Given its strong share price performance, we think it's worthwhile for Forte Minerals shareholders to consider whether its cash burn is concerning. In this report, we will consider the company's annual negative free cash flow, henceforth referring to it as the 'cash burn'. First, we'll determine its cash runway by comparing its cash burn with its cash reserves.

This technology could replace computers: discover the 20 stocks are working to make quantum computing a reality.

Advertisement

Does Forte Minerals Have A Long Cash Runway?

A company's cash runway is the amount of time it would take to burn through its cash reserves at its current cash burn rate. In December 2025, Forte Minerals had CA$13m in cash, and was debt-free. Importantly, its cash burn was CA$2.7m over the trailing twelve months. So it had a cash runway of about 4.9 years from December 2025. There's no doubt that this is a reassuringly long runway. Depicted below, you can see how its cash holdings have changed over time.

View our latest analysis for Forte Minerals

How Is Forte Minerals' Cash Burn Changing Over Time?

Because Forte Minerals isn't currently generating revenue, we consider it an early-stage business. So while we can't look to sales to understand growth, we can look at how the cash burn is changing to understand how expenditure is trending over time. In fact, it ramped its spending strongly over the last year, increasing cash burn by 146%. With spending growing that quickly, shareholders will be hoping that the money is prudently spent. Forte Minerals makes us a little nervous due to its lack of substantial operating revenue. So we'd generally prefer stocks from this list of stocks that have analysts forecasting growth.

How Easily Can Forte Minerals Raise Cash?

While Forte Minerals does have a solid cash runway, its cash burn trajectory may have some shareholders thinking ahead to when the company may need to raise more cash. Companies can raise capital through either debt or equity. Commonly, a business will sell new shares in itself to raise cash and drive growth. By comparing a company's annual cash burn to its total market capitalisation, we can estimate roughly how many shares it would have to issue in order to run the company for another year (at the same burn rate).

Forte Minerals has a market capitalisation of CA$85m and burnt through CA$2.7m last year, which is 3.2% of the company's market value. Given that is a rather small percentage, it would probably be really easy for the company to fund another year's growth by issuing some new shares to investors, or even by taking out a loan.

Is Forte Minerals' Cash Burn A Worry?

As you can probably tell by now, we're not too worried about Forte Minerals' cash burn. For example, we think its cash runway suggests that the company is on a good path. Although we do find its increasing cash burn to be a bit of a negative, once we consider the other metrics mentioned in this article together, the overall picture is one we are comfortable with. Looking at all the measures in this article, together, we're not worried about its rate of cash burn; the company seems well on top of its medium-term spending needs. On another note, Forte Minerals has 4 warning signs (and 3 which are potentially serious) we think you should know about.

For Tractor Supply, known for serving rural and suburban customers with farm, ranch and pet supplies, pet care is already a core traffic driver. VIP Petcare’s mobile clinics fit into long running trends of higher pet spending and growing demand for convenient, lower cost vet services. Bringing these clinics under the Tractor Supply umbrella could make stores a more regular stop for pet owners who already visit for food, treats and supplies.

For you as an investor, this move adds another service leg to Tractor Supply’s omnichannel model, which already includes physical stores and digital ordering options. As the integration progresses, key things to watch will be how quickly VIP Petcare offerings are rolled out across the store base and how customers respond to combining shopping trips with on site pet healthcare.

data.title: CPI Europe (WBAG:CPI) Q1 Net Margin Strength Tests Bullish Earnings Narratives
data.description:
Advertisement

Q1 2026 results set the tone

CPI Europe (WBAG:CPI) opened 2026 with Q1 revenue of €191.7 million and basic EPS of €0.76, setting a clear marker for how the new year is starting after a busy 2025. The company has seen quarterly revenue move from €188.8 million and EPS of €0.38 in Q1 2025 through €204.6 million and EPS of €2.00 in Q4 2025 to the latest print, giving investors a tight view on how the top and bottom line are tracking across recent periods. With trailing twelve month EPS at €4.11 and net margins reported at a high level, the focus now shifts to how durable those margins look against the underlying drivers.

See our full analysis for CPI Europe.

With the headline numbers on the table, the next step is to set them against the most widely held narratives around CPI Europe to see which stories hold up and which start to look stretched.

Curious how numbers become stories that shape markets? Explore Community Narratives

TTM net margin jumps to 74.1%

  • Trailing twelve month net income of €566.7 million on €765.2 million of revenue gives a 74.1% net margin, compared with 16.3% in the prior year period and helped by a €354.9 million one off gain included in the last 12 months.
  • What stands out for the bullish view is that earnings grew very strongly over the past year while revenue across the last six reported quarters stayed in a fairly tight band between €180.2 million and €223.0 million. This heavily supports the idea of strong profitability, yet the reliance on a one off gain and weak interest coverage directly challenges the idea that this level of margin is a simple, repeatable outcome.
    • The bullish angle leans on trailing EPS of €4.11 and the step up from quarterly EPS figures like €0.26 in Q3 2025 and €0.38 in Q1 2025, but that sits alongside forecasts that earnings could decline sharply over the next three years.
    • Critics point out that interest payments are not well covered by earnings and that the €354.9 million one off gain is a major driver of the 74.1% margin, so any assessment of profit strength needs to separate recurring performance from that single item.
Over the last year, CPI Europe’s profits look exceptionally strong on paper, but the mix of a very high margin, a big one off gain and weak interest coverage means the quality of those profits is just as important as the headline size when you are weighing the bullish story.📊 Read the what the Community is saying about CPI Europe.

Low 3.9x P/E versus peers

  • CPI Europe is trading on a trailing P/E of about 3.9x, compared with 13x for the wider European real estate industry and 13.6x for peers, while the share price of €15.96 also sits around 32.6% below a DCF fair value estimate of €23.69.
  • Bulls argue that such a low multiple on €4.11 of trailing EPS and a share price well below the DCF fair value suggests the stock is priced cautiously. The tension comes from forecasts that earnings could decline by around 40% per year over the next three years, which would make the current low P/E less straightforward as a sign of long term cheapness.
    • On the supportive side, trailing twelve month revenue of €765.2 million and net income of €566.7 million mean the company screens as highly profitable on recent numbers, which is often what investors look for when they see a low P/E.
    • On the cautious side, the presence of that €354.9 million one off gain and weak interest coverage gives a clear reason why some investors might see the discount to DCF fair value and peers as compensation for risk rather than a simple mispricing.

Quarterly EPS swings are wide

  • Over the last five reported quarters, basic EPS moved from €0.38 in Q1 2025 to €1.09 in Q2, €0.26 in Q3, €2.00 in Q4 and €0.76 in Q1 2026, while quarterly net income ranged between €35.4 million and €275.6 million over the same stretch.
  • What is surprising for anyone leaning bullish is how wide those swings are, because it means the strong trailing twelve month EPS of €4.11 is built on very different quarterly contributions. That pattern lines up with the idea that non recurring items play a big role and that analysts’ projections of future earnings declines are responding to that lumpiness rather than to a smooth growth trend.
    • The spread from €52.5 million of net income in Q1 2025 to €275.6 million in Q4 2025 shows how a single period can have an outsized effect on full year numbers, especially when large gains are booked.
    • For a beginner investor, this is a reminder to compare the more typical quarters such as Q1 2025, Q3 2025 and Q1 2026 against the standout Q4 2025 figure, rather than assuming the largest quarter is a new normal.

Next Steps

Most Popular Narrative: 16.8% Overvalued

Compared with the last close at $279.20, the most followed narrative pegs McDonald's fair value at $238.97, implying a meaningful valuation gap based on its fundamentals.

To calculate the intrinsic value of the company I'll use multiple methods:

• Discounted Cash Flows (DCF): intrinsic value is estimated by projecting its free cash flows over the next 10 years and discounting them to present value using the estimated cost of capital;

• EPS Growth: the fair value is estimated by projecting the Earnings Per Share CAGR for the next 5 years and then, given its current and historic values of P/E, coming up with a P/E for the 5th year. This gives an estimated price 5 years from now using the formula Price = EPS x P/E, which is then discounted using the estimated cost of capital;

• Historical P/E: we assume mean reversion to the historical P/E values;

• Historical EV/EBITDA: we assume mean reversion to the historical EV/EBITDA values;

• Historical P/S: we assume mean reversion to the historical P/S values;

• DDM (Variable): the fair value is estimated by projecting the dividend payments across the following years and discounting them to the present value using the estimated cost of capital;

• Historical Dividend Yield: we assume mean reversion to the historical dividend yield.

data.title: Grande Portage Resources (CVE:GPG) Is In A Good Position To Deliver On Growth Plans
data.description:
We can readily understand why investors are attracted to unprofitable companies. For example, although software-as-a-service business Salesforce.com lost money for years while it grew recurring revenue, if you held shares since 2005, you'd have done very well indeed. But the harsh reality is that very many loss making companies burn through all their cash and go bankrupt.

So should Grande Portage Resources (CVE:GPG) shareholders be worried about its cash burn? For the purpose of this article, we'll define cash burn as the amount of cash the company is spending each year to fund its growth (also called its negative free cash flow). We'll start by comparing its cash burn with its cash reserves in order to calculate its cash runway.

AI is about to change healthcare. These 20 stocks are working on everything from early diagnostics to drug discovery. The best part - they are all under $10bn in marketcap - there is still time to get in early.

Advertisement

Does Grande Portage Resources Have A Long Cash Runway?

You can calculate a company's cash runway by dividing the amount of cash it has by the rate at which it is spending that cash. In January 2026, Grande Portage Resources had CA$9.6m in cash, and was debt-free. Importantly, its cash burn was CA$2.6m over the trailing twelve months. Therefore, from January 2026 it had 3.7 years of cash runway. A runway of this length affords the company the time and space it needs to develop the business. Depicted below, you can see how its cash holdings have changed over time.

Check out our latest analysis for Grande Portage Resources

How Is Grande Portage Resources' Cash Burn Changing Over Time?

Grande Portage Resources didn't record any revenue over the last year, indicating that it's an early stage company still developing its business. Nonetheless, we can still examine its cash burn trajectory as part of our assessment of its cash burn situation. In fact, it ramped its spending strongly over the last year, increasing cash burn by 103%. It's fair to say that sort of rate of increase cannot be maintained for very long, without putting pressure on the balance sheet. Grande Portage Resources makes us a little nervous due to its lack of substantial operating revenue. So we'd generally prefer stocks from this list of stocks that have analysts forecasting growth.

How Hard Would It Be For Grande Portage Resources To Raise More Cash For Growth?

While Grande Portage Resources does have a solid cash runway, its cash burn trajectory may have some shareholders thinking ahead to when the company may need to raise more cash. Generally speaking, a listed business can raise new cash through issuing shares or taking on debt. Many companies end up issuing new shares to fund future growth. By looking at a company's cash burn relative to its market capitalisation, we gain insight on how much shareholders would be diluted if the company needed to raise enough cash to cover another year's cash burn.

Since it has a market capitalisation of CA$61m, Grande Portage Resources' CA$2.6m in cash burn equates to about 4.3% of its market value. Given that is a rather small percentage, it would probably be really easy for the company to fund another year's growth by issuing some new shares to investors, or even by taking out a loan.

Is Grande Portage Resources' Cash Burn A Worry?

As you can probably tell by now, we're not too worried about Grande Portage Resources' cash burn. In particular, we think its cash runway stands out as evidence that the company is well on top of its spending. While we must concede that its increasing cash burn is a bit worrying, the other factors mentioned in this article provide great comfort when it comes to the cash burn. After taking into account the various metrics mentioned in this report, we're pretty comfortable with how the company is spending its cash, as it seems on track to meet its needs over the medium term.
